title: We replaced our QA team with AI. 6 months in — honest review
post:
Six months ago, we replaced our QA team with AI tools.

I'll be honest: it was a decision driven by cost, not innovation. We couldn't afford the headcount, and the tools had gotten good enough to try.

The results? Complicated.

AI catches regressions faster and more consistently than humans ever did. Our test coverage went up 40%. Deployment frequency doubled.

But we lost something too. The intuition that a seasoned QA engineer brings. The conversations about user experience that happened during testing. The sense of ownership.

Now we're hiring one senior QA person back — someone who works alongside AI, not instead of it.

My takeaway: AI is great for regression testing. It's not great for understanding what users actually need.

What's your QA setup look like? Thoughts?
